---
title: "Part 3: Interactive Dashboards with Shiny for Python"
subtitle: "GISCI 343, Week 6"
author:
  - name: Hyesop Shin
    orcid: 0000-0003-2637-7933
    email: hyesop.shin@auckland.ac.nz
    affiliations:
      - name: University of Auckland
        address: 23 Symonds Street
        city: Auckland
        postal-code: 1010
date: 04/20/2026
date-format: long
format:
  revealjs:
    theme: default
    slide-number: true
    code-line-numbers: false
    highlight-style: github
    transition: slide
    logo: Figs/uoa-logo.png
    css: Figs/logo.css
    #width: 1280
    #height: 720
    #fontsize: 1.6em
    code-block-height: 480px
    footer: "GISCI 343 | Week 6 | Shiny for Python"
---

## Today's plan

| Time | Topic |
|------|-------|
| 13:00 to 14:00 | Lecture: from static maps to reactive dashboards |
| 14:00 to 15:00 | Guest lecture: Alex Raichev (MRCagney) |

---

## The problem with static maps

In Part 2 you made choropleth maps of Auckland.

They told a clear story, but the reader was stuck with **whatever you chose**:

- one time period
- one classification
- one colour scheme

. . .

> "What does it look like for just the central suburbs?"

Your only option: reopen the notebook and rerun.

---

## What if the user could choose?

**Assignment 1** (static):

```python
sa2.plot(column="density", legend=True)
plt.savefig("map.png")   # fixed image, one view
```

. . .

**Assignment 2** (interactive):

```python
# User picks a date range   -> map updates
# User chooses a metric     -> colours change
# User filters by category  -> everything refreshes
# All in real time, in their browser
```

---

## Why Shiny?

**Traditional web development** (HTML + JavaScript):

```javascript
document.getElementById("map").addEventListener("click",
  function(e) {
    // 50 lines of wiring code ...
  }
);
```

. . .

**Shiny** (Python):

```python
@render.plot
def chart():
    return filtered().plot.bar(x="suburb", y="population")
```

Write Python, get a web app. Shiny handles HTML, CSS, JavaScript, and browser communication for you.

---

## Shiny mental model {.smaller}

```
+------------------------------------------+
|  Browser (what the user sees)            |
|  +----------------+  +-----------------+ |
|  |    Inputs      |  |    Outputs      | |
|  |  [ Slider  ]   |  |   [ Map    ]    | |
|  |  [ Select  ]   |  |   [ Chart  ]    | |
|  |  [ Checkbox]   |  |   [ Table  ]    | |
|  +----------------+  +-----------------+ |
+------------------------------------------+
          |                    ^
          v                    |
+------------------------------------------+
|  Python server (your code)               |
|                                          |
|  @reactive.calc    -->  @render.plot     |
|  def filtered():        def chart():    |
|      ...                    ...         |
+------------------------------------------+
```

The user touches the left side. Your Python fills the right side. Shiny manages the arrows.

---

## Three components of every Shiny app {.smaller}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}
```python
from shiny import App, ui, render

# 1. UI: what the user sees
app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.h2("Hello Shiny"),
    ui.input_slider("n", "Pick a number",
                    0, 100, 50),
    ui.output_text("echo"),
)

# 2. Server: how outputs are computed
def server(input, output, session):
    @render.text
    def echo():
        return f"You picked {input.n()}"

# 3. App object: glues them together
app = App(app_ui, server)
```
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}

| Piece | Job |
|-------|-----|
| **UI** | Inputs + output placeholders |
| **Server** | Python logic that fills the outputs |
| **App** | Connects them so Shiny can run |

That is a **complete, runnable** app.
:::

:::

---

## The reactive loop

```
input.n()  (user moves slider)
    |
    v
echo()  recalculates automatically
    |
    v
Browser text updates
```

You did **not** write any code to detect the change.

Shiny noticed that `echo` reads `input.n()`, and that is enough.

. . .

This is **reactive programming**, the single idea that makes dashboards tractable.

---

## The shape of a Shiny project

```
myapp/
+-- app.py              # the app itself
+-- pyproject.toml      # dependencies
+-- data/               # CSV, GeoJSON, GeoPackage
+-- www/                # static assets (images, CSS)
+-- README.md
```

Two conventions:

- Anything in `www/` is served at `/your-file.png`
- Shiny looks for the variable called **`app`**. Rename it and Shiny will not find it.

---

## Scaffolding with `shiny create`

```bash
shiny create
```

Pick a template:

| Template | What you get |
|----------|-------------|
| `basic-app` | One input, one output |
| `basic-navigation` | Multi-page navbar |
| `dashboard` | Sidebar + cards |

Each drops a folder with a working `app.py` you can run immediately.

---

## Running the app

Three options (most common first):

1. **Positron / VS Code**: click the green "Run Shiny App" button
2. **Terminal**: `shiny run --reload app.py`
3. **Custom port**: `shiny run --port 8080 --reload app.py`

. . .

`--reload` = restart on every save. Always use it while developing.

Stop with `Ctrl+C`.



## Core vs Express syntax

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Core (we use this)
```python
app_ui = ui.page_fluid(
    ui.input_slider("n", "N", 0, 100, 50),
    ui.output_text("echo"),
)

def server(input, output, session):
    @render.text
    def echo():
        return f"{input.n()}"

app = App(app_ui, server)
```
:::

::: {.column width="50%"}
### Express (shorter, less control)
```python
from shiny.express import input, render, ui

ui.input_slider("n", "N", 0, 100, 50)

@render.text
def echo():
    return f"{input.n()}"
```
:::

:::

. . .

**Stick to Core.** It scales better and matches what the R community expects.

---

## Input widgets: what the user touches

**Numbers:**

```python
ui.input_slider("hour", "Hour:", min=0, max=23, value=12)
ui.input_numeric("count", "Count:", value=100)
```

**Choices:**

```python
ui.input_select("day", "Day:", choices=["Mon", "Tue", "Wed"])
ui.input_radio_buttons("view", "View:", choices=["Map", "Chart"])
ui.input_checkbox("weekend", "Include weekends?", value=True)
ui.input_checkbox_group("modes", "Modes:", choices=["Bus", "Train", "Ferry"])
```

**Actions:**

```python
ui.input_action_button("go", "Recompute")
```

---

## Output types: what the user sees

```python
# Text
ui.output_text("message")

# Plot (matplotlib)
ui.output_plot("chart")

# Table
ui.output_table("summary")
ui.output_data_frame("detail")    # interactive, sortable

# ipyleaflet map (via shinywidgets)
from shinywidgets import output_widget
output_widget("map")
```

Each placeholder waits for a matching function in the server.

## Now let's roll our sleeves

Open your positron.

Visit the python gis webbook and click 3.1

Go to the heading starting with Scaffolding with `shiny create`

With your group members, solve Q1 and Q2.



## Wiring inputs to outputs

```python
def server(input, output, session):

    @render.text
    def message():
        count = input.count()
        if count > 500:
            return f"{count}: high traffic"
        return f"{count}: normal traffic"
```

Key rule: **call inputs with parentheses**.

```python
input.hour     # the function object (wrong)
input.hour()   # the current value   (right)
```

---

## Three kinds of reactive node

```{mermaid}
%%| fig-width: 10
graph LR
    A["input.city()"] --> C["@reactive.calc<br/>filtered()"]
    B["input.min_pop()"] --> C
    C --> D["@render.table<br/>tbl"]
    C --> E["@render.plot<br/>chart"]
    F["input.log()"] --> G["@reactive.effect<br/>log click"]
    C -.-> G
```

| Kind | Example | Behaviour |
|------|---------|-----------|
| **Source** | `input.x()`, `reactive.value()` | Changes from outside; no dependencies |
| **Expression** | `@reactive.calc` | Lazy, cached, returns a value |
| **Observer** | `@render.*`, `@reactive.effect` | Eager, runs for side effects |

---

## `@reactive.calc`: calculate once, reuse everywhere

```python
@reactive.calc
def filtered():
    df = data[data["city"] == input.city()]
    return df[df["population"] >= input.min_pop()]
```

- Define **inside** `server`
- **Call with parentheses**: `filtered()`
- Result is **cached**: call it ten times, runs once

. . .

Rule of thumb: if two outputs do the same filter, lift it into a `@reactive.calc`.

---

## Without `@reactive.calc` (inefficient)

```python
@render.plot
def plot1():
    data = load_and_process()   # slow!
    return make_plot(data)

@render.table
def table1():
    data = load_and_process()   # slow again!
    return data
```

. . .

## With `@reactive.calc` (efficient)

```python
@reactive.calc
def processed():
    return load_and_process()   # once!

@render.plot
def plot1():
    return make_plot(processed())   # reuses cache

@render.table
def table1():
    return processed()              # reuses cache
```

---

## `@reactive.effect`: for things the user does not see

```python
@reactive.effect
def log_year():
    print(f"[{input.year()}] user changed the year filter")
```

Same dependency tracking, but:

- **No return value**
- **No caching**
- Runs for side effects (logging, writing a file)

. . .

If the user needs to **see** it, use `@render.*`, not `@reactive.effect`.

---

## `@reactive.event`: "only when I say so"

```python
@reactive.calc
@reactive.event(input.go)
def total():
    return input.a() + input.b()
```

Move the sliders: nothing happens.

Click "Recompute": `total` updates.

. . .

Perfect for expensive computations where the user should control the timing.

---

## Quick reference {.smaller}

| Decorator | Returns value? | Cached? | When does it run? |
|-----------|:-:|:-:|-------------------|
| `@reactive.calc` | Yes | Yes | When read, if stale |
| `@reactive.effect` | No | No | Whenever deps change |
| `@render.text` / `@render.plot` | Yes (to browser) | No | Whenever deps change |
| `@reactive.event(input.x)` | (modifier) | | Only when `input.x` fires |

---

## Worked example: suburbs explorer {.smaller}

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="55%"}
```python
@reactive.calc
def filtered():
    df = data[data["city"] == input.city()]
    return df[df["population"] >= input.min_pop()]

@render.table
def tbl():
    return filtered()

@render.plot
def chart():
    df = filtered()
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7, 4))
    ax.barh(df["suburb"], df["median_income"],
            color="steelblue")
    ax.set_xlabel("Median income")
    return fig

@reactive.effect
@reactive.event(input.log)
def _():
    print(f"[LOG] {input.city()}, "
          f"n={len(filtered())} rows")
```
:::

::: {.column width="45%"}
**What happens:**

- Change city or slider: table + chart redraw
- Click "Log": message prints, nothing visible changes
- `filtered()` runs **once** per change, shared by both outputs
:::

:::

---

## Walk through the reactive graph

- **Two sources**: `input.city()` and `input.min_pop()`
- **One expression**: `filtered()`, cached and shared
- **Two render observers**: `tbl` and `chart`, redraw when `filtered` changes
- **One effect observer**: `_`, gated by `@reactive.event(input.log)`, only the button triggers it

. . .

This pattern (shared calc, multiple render outputs, one gated effect) covers most dashboards you will ever build.

---

## ipyleaflet + shinywidgets (for next week in the lab) {.smaller}

```python
from shinywidgets import output_widget, render_widget
from ipyleaflet import Map, GeoData
import geopandas as gpd

layer = gpd.read_file("data/sa2.geojson").to_crs(4326)

# UI
output_widget("map")

# Server
@render_widget
def map():
    m = Map(center=(-36.85, 174.77), zoom=11)
    m.add_layer(GeoData(
        geo_dataframe=filtered_layer(),
        style={"color": "#d73027", "weight": 2,
               "fillOpacity": 0.4},
    ))
    return m
```

Two things to remember:

- **CRS**: ipyleaflet needs WGS84 (`EPSG:4326`)
- **Layer refresh**: return a **new** `Map` each time inside `@render_widget`

---

## Layout: sidebar + cards

```python
app_ui = ui.page_sidebar(
    ui.sidebar(
        ui.input_select("city", "City", choices=cities),
        ui.input_slider("min_pop", "Min population",
                        0, 20000, 0, step=1000),
    ),
    ui.card(
        ui.card_header("Chart"),
        ui.output_plot("chart"),
    ),
    ui.card(
        ui.card_header("Map"),
        output_widget("map"),
    ),
    title="Auckland dashboard",
)
```

Cards group related content. Sidebar keeps inputs out of the way.

---

## Debugging: four techniques that solve 90% of problems

**1. Read the traceback** (last line first)

```
AttributeError: 'Inputs' object has no attribute 'n'
```

You wrote `input.n` instead of `input.n()`, or the id does not match.

. . .

**2. `print()` is your friend**

```python
@render.text
def echo():
    print(f"echo running with n={input.n()}")
    return f"You picked {input.n()}"
```

If it does not print, the function is not running.

---

## Debugging (continued)

**3. Silent failures = name mismatches**

```python
ui.output_text("echo")     # placeholder: "echo"

@render.text
def eccho():                # function: "eccho" (typo!)
    ...                     # blank output, no error
```

. . .

**4. Test your functions outside Shiny first**

```python
# In a notebook or script
data = pd.read_csv("data/test.csv")
result = your_filter_function(data, "Auckland")
print(result.head())       # does it work?
```

If the function is correct, the Shiny wiring is the only suspect.

---

## Deploying with shinylive: no server needed

**Traditional web app:**

```
User  -->  Server (running Python)  -->  Response
             (costs money, needs maintenance)
```

. . .

**Shinylive (WebAssembly):**

```
User  -->  Browser (runs Python via Pyodide)  -->  Response
             (free, no server, works offline)
```

Your app becomes static HTML files hosted on **GitHub Pages** for free.


## Quiz 3: Shiny Dashboard Exploration {.smaller}

1. Load your Shiny dashboard-tip in the browser using the code: <br> `shiny create --template dashboard-tips`
3. Render the template using <br> `shiny run --reload [dashboard folder path]/app.py`
4. Answer the Canvas question


## Key takeaways {.smaller}

1. A Shiny app has three pieces: **UI**, **server**, **App**
2. **Reactive programming**: describe what depends on what, Shiny handles when
3. Three reactive nodes: **sources** (inputs), **expressions** (`@reactive.calc`), **observers** (`@render.*`, `@reactive.effect`)
4. `@reactive.calc` = cached shared value; `@reactive.effect` = side effects; `@reactive.event` = gated trigger
5. `ipyleaflet` maps go inside Shiny via `output_widget` + `@render_widget`
6. `shinylive export . docs` turns your app into a free static site

---

## After the break: Alex Raichev (MRCagney)

Alex will talk about how interactive dashboards are used in real transport consulting work in Auckland.

**Expect:**

- Examples of dashboards MRCagney has built for clients
- What questions clients actually ask of a dashboard, vs a static report
- What separates a useful dashboard from a beautiful but unread one

. . .

Take notes. The design report will be stronger if you can connect your design choices to professional practice.

---

## Further reading

- Posit, [Shiny for Python: Create and run](https://shiny.posit.co/py/get-started/create-run.html)
- Posit, [Shiny for Python: Debug, troubleshoot, and help](https://shiny.posit.co/py/get-started/debug.html)
- Posit, [Reactivity patterns](https://shiny.posit.co/py/docs/reactive-patterns.html)
- Wickham, [Mastering Shiny, Ch 1](https://mastering-shiny.org/basic-app.html) and [Ch 3](https://mastering-shiny.org/action-intro.html)
- [Shinylive examples gallery](https://shinylive.io/py/examples/)
